note: Watched a team spend 20 minutes in a meeting arguing about why the pipeline report was wrong. Turned out two people had different ideas of what 'closed' meant. The report was fine. The definitions weren't.
Cleaned up a HubSpot portal today with 47 lifecycle stage values. Forty-seven. Half were typos or one-offs someone added for a single campaign in 2022 and never removed.
A client asked me to build a complicated lead-scoring model. I asked when they last looked at their current one. Nobody could remember. We turned it off for a month and the sales numbers didn't move.
Spent the morning untangling a workflow that fired three emails to the same person because someone had built three separate workflows all watching the same property. Nobody owned the overlap.
The most useful thing I did this week was delete things. 12 unused properties, 5 dead workflows, a dashboard nobody had opened in a year. CRM hygiene is mostly subtraction.

Opened a CRM where 99% of contacts had no owner. You can't run a single rep-level report on that, no accountability, no territory cuts, nothing. Spent a day backfilling owner from the majority deal owner on each company. Coverage went from 1% to 23% and suddenly the reports actually meant something. Ownership is the backbone everyone forgets until it's missing.

Two reps' meetings weren't showing up in any report. They were logging them as plain notes instead of meeting activities. Instead of nagging them to change how they work, I wrote a job that reads the note, figures out what kind of meeting it was, and creates the activity behind the scenes. Meet people where they are; fix it in code.

'Meetings booked this week' was double-counting and nobody could figure out why. Reps log meetings days after they happen, so the create date is today but the meeting actually occurred last Tuesday. The report keyed off create date, so every retro-logged meeting inflated the number. Know which date your report is actually counting.

Built a property, backfilled it across a couple thousand records, then deleted it a week later. No workflow could keep it current, and a field that's quietly wrong is worse than no field, because people trust it and they shouldn't. If you can't keep it accurate automatically, don't ship it.

A manager wanted to coach a rep for low meeting numbers. The problem: the meeting report was broken. You can't hold someone accountable to a number they can correctly prove is wrong. Fix the data first, then have the performance conversation. A bad report doesn't just mislead, it hands people a valid excuse.

A sync ran overnight and silently overwrote the owner field on a big chunk of the contact database. No error, no alert, just wrong data by morning. Now every integration I build keeps an audit trail and a way to restore the previous value. Assume your syncs will clobber something eventually, because they will.

Built a dedup system and learned the real lesson the hard way: dedup is not a project you finish. New duplicates show up every day from forms, imports, and integrations. If it isn't running on a schedule, you're mopping the floor with the faucet still on.

Built a nightly job that scores a few thousand accounts on a dozen signals: engagement, deal velocity, days since last order. The clever model wasn't the point. The point was that a human used to eyeball this for the top 20 accounts and ignore the other few thousand. Automation's real value is coverage, not cleverness.

Everyone wants an AI agent running their CRM. Nobody wants to talk about the database it's reading from. Point an agent at stale stages and duplicate accounts and it doesn't fix anything, it just makes the wrong call faster, at 3am, with no one watching. Autonomy amplifies whatever's already in the data. Clean it before you hand over the keys.

The lead-scoring model, the forecast, the AI insights: they're all just a mirror of the data underneath. Feed a model a CRM where a third of the records are stale and you don't get bad luck, you get confident, well-formatted wrong answers. The bottleneck was never the algorithm. It's the records nobody has touched in a year.

Watched a team try to fix a reporting problem by buying a sixth tool. The problem wasn't a missing tool. 'Qualified lead' already meant three different things across the five tools they had. Every system you bolt on is one more place your definitions can fork. Most stacks don't need another app. They need someone to decide what the words mean.

Bought contact data has a half-life. The title was right the day you enriched it. Eighteen months later the person changed jobs and your sequence is greeting a stranger. The data you actually own, what your own forms and calls and product captured, is the only data that doesn't rot on someone else's schedule. Spend more on what you capture than on what you buy.

No one's CRM problem has ever been 'not enough seats.' But that's where the money goes: more licenses, another module, while the data layer holding it all up gets nothing. If half your records are wrong, a bigger CRM just gives you more room to be wrong in. Fund the plumbing. It's the least exciting line item and the only one that pays you back.

RevOps keeps getting sold as a transformation. A framework, a maturity curve, a forty-slide deck. The actual wins this quarter: fixed a date filter, deleted a handful of dead workflows, got owner coverage up so the reports finally meant something. None of that goes on a conference stage. It's also the only thing that moved a number.

A forecast is only as honest as the stage data feeding it. If reps move deals to commit late and let closed-lost sit untouched, the number on the board is fiction with a confidence interval. You don't fix a bad forecast in the forecast. You fix it three steps upstream, in whether the pipeline reflects what's actually happening.

The fastest way to get clean activity data is to stop asking reps to type it in. Every required field is one that gets logged late, half-filled, or skipped, and then you build a forecast on the gaps. Capture it from the source: the email, the calendar, the call recording. Reps should sell. The system should do the paperwork.

A 12-person company asked me which HubSpot tier they needed to fix their problems. They were using about 4% of the one they already paid for. Most small teams don't have a tool problem, they have an adoption problem, and a bigger plan doesn't fix that.

Watched a founder personally chase every lead out of a shared inbox because 'the CRM was too much.' We set up one pipeline, three stages, and a single automation that logged the emails for him. He didn't change how he worked; the system just started remembering for him. Small teams don't need more CRM. They need less of it, set up right.

A small-business owner told me HubSpot 'didn't do anything.' They'd bought it, imported a spreadsheet, and never built a single workflow. The software isn't the product. The fifteen decisions about how your business actually runs are the product. The tool just enforces them.

The most expensive CRM mistake I see at small companies isn't the license. It's the owner spending Saturday rebuilding the same report by hand because nobody set the pipeline up to answer the question once. Your time is the budget. Spend an afternoon now or every weekend forever.

A 20-person shop wanted lead scoring 'like the big companies have.' They got 300 leads a month. You don't need a model to rank 300 leads, you need someone to actually call them. Half of 'we need automation' is really 'we haven't decided who owns the follow-up.'
